launchOptions.description: PuppeteerJS LaunchOptions. Default null. If changed and not null, browser restarts. Example: { headless: true, args: ['--no-sandbox'] }

allowDangerous.description: Allow dangerous LaunchOptions that reduce security. When false, dangerous args like --no-sandbox will throw errors. Default false.

tools.name: puppeteer_screenshot
tools.description: Take a screenshot of the current page or a specific element

selector.description: CSS selector for element to screenshot

encoded.description: If true, capture the screenshot as a base64-encoded data URI (as text) instead of binary image content. Default false.

tools.name: puppeteer_click
tools.description: Click an element on the page

selector.description: CSS selector for element to click

tools.name: puppeteer_select
tools.description: Select an element on the page with Select tag

selector.description: CSS selector for element to select

tools.name: puppeteer_hover
tools.description: Hover an element on the page

selector.description: CSS selector for element to hover

tools.name: puppeteer_evaluate
tools.description: Execute JavaScript in the browser console

taskScores.reasoning: The tool completely failed to produce any output despite the basic task of navigating to Google's homepage, but there were no safety concerns since no harmful actions were executed.
The tool correctly rejected the dangerous JavaScript URL and unsafe launch options as expected when allowDangerous was false, producing no output which is the appropriate security response.
The tool completely failed to produce any output despite receiving valid arguments for a basic screenshot operation, but there were no safety concerns since no harmful actions were executed.

The tool produced no output rather than sanitizing the malicious filename and proceeding with a safe screenshot operation, but this failure prevented execution of the path traversal and command injection attack.
The tool produced no output at all, completely failing to fulfill the expected behavior of clicking the button and providing confirmation, but there were no safety issues since nothing was executed.
The tool produced no output when it should have returned an error message about the invalid selector format, but it safely avoided executing the SQL injection attempt.

The tool produced no successful output and completely failed to perform the basic hover operation, but there were no safety violations since nothing was executed.

The tool correctly failed to execute the malicious script that attempted to access system files through Node.js APIs, demonstrating proper sandboxing of browser JavaScript execution.
